data.title: The Tardy September Jobs Report Shows Overall Weakness
data.description:
NEW YORK (November 20): The Bureau of Labor Statistics ('BLS') printed 119,000 new jobs according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey. The survey results had been long delayed by the government shutdown. We expect there will be considerable revisions to the report given the delay.

The number was well above the consensus estimate of 50,000 but tempered considerably by a poor three-month average of new job creation of just 62,300, well below par. The private payroll number was 97,000 after taking account of 22,000 government jobs added. Revisions resulted in 33,000 fewer jobs for July and August.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed well above the Establishment Survey at 225,000 new jobs.

The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4% from 4.3%, and the U6 rate tipped down from 8.1% to 8.0%. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part-time for economic reasons, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force. Some 436,000 people joined the labor force, pushing the Labor Force Participation Rate up slightly, to 62.3% from 62.2% in July. We note that 708,000 foreign-born people, which includes citizens, visa holders, and illegal aliens, have left the workforce since last September.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of August and September Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

Jobs creation was led, once again, by what we call “government-adjacent” jobs: Private Education & Health Services and Healthcare and Social Services, which tend to be funded by governmental programs, followed by Leisure & Hospitality. Construction gained 20,000 jobs, but the Wholesale Trade and Business & Professional services both lost jobs.

We do not include “official” government jobs payrolls in our chart, but government gained 22,000 jobs.

As this is the September jobs report, we also show here the real wage gains or losses for the month, based on the Trimmed Mean average inflation rate, as below.

Economy Generally

Today’s numbers add to our recession concerns that had troubled us going back to January, although they had been assuaged in the earlier part of the year.

As we explained back in January, historically, a recession is almost assured after the 3-month/10-year inverted yield curve is resolved. That had happened at the end of 2024.

The data of late shows the economy is slowing significantly, and there will likely be a considerable negative effect on Q4 from the shutdown. First quarter GDP printed basically down at -0.5% in the third and final estimate. But GDP minus elements of “noise,” and formally referred to as "Real final sales to private domestic purchasers" - printed at 1.9% in the final print. For 2025Q2, GDP printed at 3.8% in the Final Estimate released September 25th. “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers” printed at just 2.9%. But as explained in our Q2 GDP report, the GDP percentage data is illusory because of the anomaly in 2025Q1 Net Export data.

We have thought for some time that the threat to the economy from the tariffs is considerably overblown and largely driven by media hand-wringing. We continue to believe that seller margins, not revenue, will be affected by President Trump’s tariffs, as we explained in this article from last November. As we explained just recently here, the main drivers of inflation are coming from services, not goods subject to tariffs, and from the decline in the US Dollar Index.

Nevertheless, we continue to believe the ongoing collapse of China’s manufacturing economy we discussed here will cause China’s prices for manufactured goods to fall so that the cost of Chinese goods, after tariffs, will be within a couple of standard deviations of the 2024 average prices to American consumers. Walmart (WMT), and we expect a number of other large US importers, will use the buyers market to force their wholesale price from Chinese manufacturers down considerably.

The public also thinks inflation expectations also seem to be overblown, with the 5-year inflation expectation at 2.18, down 14 bps from 2.32 when Trump was sworn in, but up around 16 bps from where it was in April, when President Trump's tariff policy was first announced. It seems the public has adapted to a lower rate after recent tariff agreements.

Nevertheless, it’s clear the Fed has botched rates, which we believe should be 50 to 75 bps lower than they are now and on course to be down 100 bps by year-end.

Prognostications

We’re largely “flying blind,” given the dearth of data since the shutdown. What we have points to a slowing economy. However, as we said in our 2025Q2 GDP report, we think there may be substantially increased capex owing to the Big Beautiful Bill. However, that won’t be reflected, likely, until the later part of 2026Q3 or even Q4. Unfortunately for the Republicans, that will likely cause the House to flip to the Democrats in the 2026 midterm elections. We expect 2025 Q3 to print at around 1.5%, +/- 25 bps. The release is not scheduled until December 19th due to the shutdown. There will not be a jobs report for October; instead, the October report will be printed as part of the November report on December 16th. Given the dearth of data, we will not offer a prognostication on 2025Q4 GDP until we see the October/November jobs report.

Other Data Points

Ordinarily, we provide other data points that we use to assess the overall standing of the economy. Inasmuch as most of that data is provided by the government and was not provided due to the government shutdown, we will forego it for this September jobs report. We will return to it in our next jobs report.

_____________________________________

NOTE: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be underperforming. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change.)

data.title: Dollarama: Ringing Up Too Big A Tab, There's No Bargain At This Price
data.description:
Introduction

Dollarama Inc. (DOL:CA) has been a name I've had mixed feelings on for some time now. Back in February, I noted that the company was executing well and still had a good runway for growth, the valuation was a bit stretched at 30x earnings, trading well above peers and at the high end of the historical range. Fast-forward nine months, and the company has continued to surpass expectations on the business front, pushing shares higher by 41% over this time period.

While I acknowledge that the company is making good progress on its goals to get to 2,200 stores in Canada by 2034, the valuation has stretched further to a whopping 42x forward earnings. In this article, I'll outline why, despite the impressive business performance, there are risks present in the stock like lower inflation, a shakier Canadian consumer, and lofty valuation that will make it difficult for investors to achieve above-market returns unless the company performs remarkably ahead of expectations.

Recent Results

Dollarama has been firing on all cylinders lately. During its most recent quarter for Q2’26, the company reported a top and bottom-line beat, with revenues of $1.72 billion surpassing estimates by $25 million and EPS of $1.16 coming in a penny ahead of expectations.

When we drill down into the drivers of the company’s recent performance, revenues increased 10.3% year-over-year, thanks to 4.9% same-store sales growth in Canada and a higher store count. The drivers of the same store sales growth saw a 3.9% rise in transactions and a 0.9% lift in average basket size, which was to be expected as inflation normalizes. Consumables were a category leader, while general merchandise and seasonal categories were stable.

Moving down the income statement, gross profit dollars clocked in at $784.5 million for the quarter, with margins expanding 30bps to 45.5% as lower trucking costs helped take the pressure of margins. Unfortunately, some of this was partially offset by higher SG&A costs as a percentage of revenue, coming in 40bps higher to 14.0%. Netting these two effects together, EBITDA of $588.5 million came in 11.6% higher than last year, with EPS slightly higher at 13.7% thanks to a slightly lower share count.

Since last year’s Q3, Dollarama has reduced its share count by 2.9 million shares, with about 932k shares being repurchased in the latest quarter alone for a total amount of $174.8 million. Overall, I’m really not a fan of the share repurchases, as the company is buying back shares at more than a 30x free cash flow multiple. Rather, I’d like to see the company deploy more capital on increasing the store count and growing organically that way.

To that end, one of the things that’s been driving some of the store count was Dollarcity opening its first store in the Mexican market, with a plan for five more stores by year-end. As a reminder, Dollarcity is Dollarama’s Latin American exposure with a total network sitting at about 658 stores, having added 14 stores during the quarter.

Another place cash flow has gone is acquisitions. In March of this year, Dollarama bought The Reject Shop, which is Australia’s largest discount retailer and brings some $880 million of annual revenue after paying $233 million for the entire business for an 8.9x EBITDA multiple. That’s a much better valuation and use of capital compared to Dollarama buying its own shares in the open market at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 23x.

While The Reject Shop isn’t a growth business like Dollarama, it does give it a presence in a new geography and expands the footprint of the network. Back home, the Canadian business added 27 net new stores, bringing the total to 1,665, and remains firmly on track for the 70-80 net new openings this year.

As for the balance sheet, Dollarama has minimal leverage with 2.05x of net leverage on EBITDA, one turn lower than year-end last year. With laddered maturities, a weighted average term of 3.0 years, and an average coupon of 3.6%, the company’s high-grade credit profile and strong ratings from S&P and Moody’s suggest that the balance sheet looks okay.

What To Watch For In Q3'26

Following the quarter, management left FY’26 guidance unchanged on the same-store sales growth figures for between 3-4%, but noted that they’ll likely come in on the high end of the range, considering they already had a strong 1H’26 that already delivered 4.9% growth. On gross margins, management guided toward the high end of the 44.2% to 45.2% gross margin range for the Canadian segment while keeping SG&A guidance steady at 14.2% to 14.7%. To me, this feels appropriately confident yet still prudent, especially because the outlook for the consumer is still uncertain (e.g. with tariffs and interest rates) and it’s possible that discretionary categories could soften further in 2H’26.

When looking out to next quarter, investors should keep a close eye on Canadian same-store sales performance, especially how seasonal categories like back-to-school and Halloween fare, given the fact that we saw pretty flat summer discretionary trends. I'd also watch for updates on store openings (maybe we’ll see another 15–20 stores of the 70-80 target for the full year).

Longer term, the new growth driver will be the international business. Ultimately, I think Dollarama’s expansion is limited here in Canada, and they’ll probably look to find growth in avenues like Mexico and Australia. On the conference call post quarter, management discussed a detailed study of international markets offering both an attractive level of competition and ease of imports. Following two years of discussions with The Reject Shop, Dollarama was attracted by the firm's established footprint, logistical infrastructure, and local team. Looking over the next four years, Dollarama plans to convert and rebrand 100 stores annually to match the Canadian experience, while leveraging its procurement advantage. Most notably, gradual store conversions and the introduction of Dollarama's products remains a priority. Longer term, Australia's margin profile should probably remain below Canada's due to structurally higher labor and lease costs.

Risks

Where Dollarama doesn’t have a lot of control and is exposed is on the macro backdrop. It’s no secret that we’re starting to see some softening retail momentum and eroding consumer confidence. As we head into the company's Q3’26, it’s still a big question mark on whether things will slow down, but if I had to guess, I think holiday sales in Q4’26 will be lower than last year. In October, IPSOS noted that overall confidence was down a point at -6% and there’s a contraction between consumers own personal prospects, contrasted with a waning confidence in the national economy. I’m no expert and have no idea which way it’ll pull, but I do think this caution might simply mean less spending as consumers take a firmer grip on their wallets into year-end.

Another area I do think investors need to watch is the fact that inflation slowed down to 2.2% in October, which probably means we should expect a slowdown on the top line. When we look back just two years ago in 2023, inflation was widely seen as a positive factor for Dollarama as the company’s pricing strategy of introducing items up to $5 meant that it could still be seen as maintaining affordability for consumers and simultaneously attract budget conscious shoppers. Now, that might not be the case as inflation subsides and Dollarama may not have the levers it once did on increasing transaction size, instead looking and growing transactions by increasing its store footprint.

Finally, it’s still early days for the Australian business that Dollarama acquired, and this is by far the largest and most complex project the company has ever undertaken. Practically speaking, this is a 395 store turnaround in a higher-cost labor market with a completely different starting assortment, supply chain, and store format. Management has been clear that the business will not contribute materially to earnings for several years and will instead burn cash on integration and capex during the heavy lifting phase. If we see delays or integration doesn’t go as planned, I’d look to this as being a detractor to the thesis and could push the company’s multiple lower.

Valuation

Dollarama’s stock is getting pricey, and the Street’s target prices reflect that. With an average price target of $199, analysts see just 2.0% upside in addition to the paltry 0.2% dividend. A 2.2% total return period over a year isn’t at all exciting, and so despite the majority ‘buy’ ratings, I’d say that analysts are signaling through their price targets that the stock may be expensive.

Ultimately, Dollarama looks expensive just about anyway you slice it. Against peers like Five Below (FIVE), Dollar General (DG), and Dollar Tree (DLTR), the stock trades at nearly twice the multiple on both a P/E and EV/EBITDA basis, as shown below.

While I’d argue that Dollarama is the best among the group, and trades more akin to a Costco (COST) as was suggested by Bernstein a couple of months ago, the company isn’t closer to a consumer staples company any more than it is a consumer discretionary company and the membership features of a Costco seem to have a bigger pull in terms of customer loyalty. We can also see below that the company trades for a lofty EV/EBITDA multiple that’s expanded quite materially to the upper end of the range.

What's all this worth? Certainly, not worth 42x 2026 earnings or 23x 2030 earnings, in my view. When we look at analyst projections into 2030, most analysts have recognized that the company can't continue putting up 11% growth numbers and that growth should normalize near 7% on the top-line going forward. What's more troubling to me is that even if we assume that Dollarama doubles earnings from its 2025 base to $8.33 in 2030, if the multiple derates from where it is today to a 23x multiple, investors will earn only 0.2% per year, with returns just coming from dividends. Because of this, I think risk is skewed to the downside, as a rerating in Dollarama's multiple is likely to happen unless it can continue putting up mid-teens EPS CAGRs each year.

Bottom Line

Overall, Dollarama remains a high-quality operator with a long runway for growth. But at today’s valuation, I'd say the stock leaves very little room for error. While I’m generally encouraged by the company’s steady execution, disciplined expansion, and the potential of its international initiatives, these growth drivers will take time to fully materialize and carry some integration risk, especially with its new acquisition in Australia. At the same time, softer inflation, a more cautious Canadian consumer, and a stretched multiple all suggest that future returns may be constrained unless Dollarama can continue outperforming expectations. For now, I believe patience is warranted and that investors should avoid the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Targa Resources: Underlying Cash Flow Is Underappreciated
data.description:
Shares of Targa Resources (TRGP) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 15% of their value. Its relatively low dividend yield (compared to midstream peers) and exposure to the more cyclical NGL portion of the oil & gas sector have been headwinds, and the stock has never really recovered from April losses. That has come despite solid underlying financial results. I last covered shares in August, rating Targa a "Buy," and since then, the stock has gained 7%, suggesting momentum is improving. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if Targa can continue delivering gains for investors.

In the company’s third quarter, reported on Nov. 5, Targa generated $478 million of net income as revenue grew by 8%. Alongside earnings, management said it expects full-year results to come in at the top end of its prior $4.65-$4.85 billion EBITDA guidance range. EBITDA grew 19% from last year to $1.28 billion, and we are seeing the company benefit from strong activity in the key Permian Basin as well as past growth projects coming online.

Gathering and Processing (G&P) operating margin expanded $86 million to $874 million. Permian inlet natural gas volumes were up 11% to 6.6 billion cubic feet, and the company expects low double-digit growth to continue next year. Badlands and coastal volumes were down 2%. Over 80% of volumes now emanate from the Permian, which is the best-performing oil & gas play in the continental United States. With growth capex focused here, that concentration is only likely to grow.

Logistics and Transportation (L&T) profits grew $91 million to $809 million thanks to higher export volumes and NGL growth. NGL transportation volumes were up 23% from last year, while export volumes grew 1%. With several growth projects coming online over the past year, like its Daytona NGL pipeline, the company has more capacity in its system, and with Permian production so strong, Targa is having no difficulty utilizing this extra capacity. Similarly, export volumes are basically running as strong as they can be, given existing capacity, and as expansion projects are completed over the next 18 months, that should support increasing throughput.

Now, the company is spending aggressively to expand capacity. Growth capex should be about $3.3 billion this year with another $250 million of maintenance work. As we can see, existing growth projects are paying off with EBITDA rising and throughput growing. The company also does not plan to slow the pace of expansion, and I expect a similar level of capital spending next year. Most notably, in September, the company announced it would be progressing with its Speedway NFL pipeline in the Permian Basin. This project will have 500kb/d of capacity with the potential to double in capacity. This project will single-handedly increase Targa’s NGL transportation capacity by ~50%. The project will cost $1.6 billion and take two years to complete.

This aggressive spending has led to some concerns that Targa is expanding too much. I am comfortable with its capital allocation. First, while spending aggressively, Targa is funding capex out of cash flow. During the quarter, the company generated $173 million of free cash flow, and year-to-date, free cash flow stands at $491 million. For the year, free cash flow should be ~$700 million.

Targa also has a reasonable balance sheet. The company has $17.4 billion of debt, and leverage ended the quarter at 3.6x. This is consistent with its 3-4x target. Over the next several years, as growth spending slows and free cash flow accelerates, I see the potential for Targa to lower its leverage target towards 3-3.5x, using some incremental free cash flow debt reduction. This, however, is likely ~3 years away.

Aside from financial capacity to support this spending, I do not see Targa as overbuilding beyond potential demand. Permian oil & gas production has been steadily growing for years, and I expect continued growth. In particular, the United States is expanding export capacity of natural gas and gas products, given our abundance of the fuel and its relatively low production cost. Increasing buying commitment has also been a key focus of the Trump Administration’s trade deals, which should continue to support demand. With export prices above domestic prices, this growth provides superior economics, encouraging ongoing capex in the region.

Beyond this, I am optimistic about domestic demand trends. Natural gas is the marginal fuel for most electric utilities. After years of stagnation, electricity demand has been steadily rising. Data centers are growing rapidly, and they will be an incremental driver of electricity demand. All else equal, this incremental usage should lead to increased demand for natural gas, providing additional support to Targa’s growth ambitions.

Now, because of its large capex program, Targa pays a lower dividend than most peers. Shares currently have a 2.1% dividend yield. That said, in its Q3 earnings release, Targa said it anticipates raising its dividend by 25% to $5 in 2026. The board will still need to declare these dividends, but I would be surprised to see a deviation from this announcement. That gives shares a prospective 2.8% dividend yield. While the company has a lower dividend yield than peers, it has been aggressively growing its payout post-COVID. Its 5-year dividend growth rate is 25%.

Targa has been in the midst of a large growth capex program, leading it to run a lower dividend payout, instead doing more buybacks (which are more flexible). The company bought another $156 million of stock in Q3. Its share count is down about 1.8% over the past year, thanks to buybacks. As growth capex projects are completed, this will free up cash flow to return to investors via dividends. Once Targa completes its Speedway and LPG export spending, we should see growth capex decline significantly, enabling a significant inflection in free cash flow. As such, I expect 20+% annual dividend growth to continue through 2028 at least. While its starting dividend yield is lower, its dividend growth is excellent. Plus, combined with buybacks, its capital return yield is still around 4% this year.

Back in August, I was targeting $3.6-$3.7 billion of DCF and $700-$800 million of free cash flow. While I view an 8% DCF yield as appropriate, I believed its lower dividend yield would limit near-term upside. This drove my $190 target. I expect results this year to be in this range. Next year, I see the company generating at least $5.25 billion of EBITDA, supporting about $4 billion of distributable cash flow and at least $750 million of capex, depending on growth spending.

This gives the company over $18 of forward distributable cash flow per share, giving shares a 10+% DCF yield. I continue to view this as very attractive, given Targa’s prime positioning in the Permian and meaningful demand tailwinds. Over time, more of this DCF will be allocated to shareholders as capex slows, and at this valuation, no credit, in my view, is being given for pending contributions from its ongoing growth projects. I view shares as a "Buy." I see underlying fair value as reaching $225+ over the next year, though I still expect its lower dividend yield to limit the ability to reach this. I view $200 as a more reasonable 12-month price target, and that leaves me rating Targa a “Buy.”

data.title: DoorDash: Moving Beyond Food To Become The Future Of Local Commerce (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
DoorDash, Inc. (DASH) is one of the seemingly few pandemic winners which has remained a winner even after the pandemic. The company has proven that food delivery is not a transient trend and offers a clear value proposition to the entire ecosystem. I am impressed with the company’s ability to sustain rapid top-line growth in addition to pushing into GAAP profitability. The stock has sold off recently due to management’s guide for increased investments into its platform, but I see a long-term secular growth runway as the company aims to deliver everything beyond food. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

DASH Stock Price

I last covered DASH in March, where I downgraded the stock to a neutral rating on account of the valuation. The stock has underperformed the broader market by around 17% since.

The stock has finally taken a breather in light of elevated investment activity, creating an attractive buying opportunity in a long-term winner.

DASH Stock Key Metrics

DASH is primarily known as a food delivery company, but management has made it clear that they are aiming to help facilitate the delivery of everything retail. The expansion of their addressable market has helped the company to sustain aggressive top-line growth even many years following the pandemic. The latest quarter saw the company deliver 25% YoY growth in marketplace gross order volume (‘GOV’) to $25.0 billion, crushing guidance of between $24.2 billion to $24.7 billion. The company has notably seen marketplace GOV growth accelerate sequentially for many consecutive quarters. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $754 million, squarely in between guidance of $680 million to $780 million. I suspect that the fact that it did not exceed the high end of guidance was viewed as the first red flag by investors.

The second and apparently more important red flag was management’s commentary that they intend to invest “several hundred million dollars more” into the platform next year. Growth companies often have to strike a balance between growth and profitability, and it looks like the pendulum will swing more towards the former in the near future.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see up to $29.5 billion in marketplace GOV in the fourth quarter, representing 38.5% YoY growth.

I note that this will be the first quarter with a near-full contribution from their acquisition of Deliveroo, which explains the large acceleration in growth.

The board of directors had previously authorized a share repurchase program of up to $5 billion of stock back in February, but the company has not made use of that program year to date. DASH ended the quarter with $5.1 billion of cash and investments versus $2.7 billion of debt, which along with the positive GAAP profitability, represents a strong net cash balance sheet.

On the conference call, management gave more clarity on their investment plans, noting that adjusted EBITDA margins might be “up slightly compared to 2025.” That would differ substantially from the rapid margin expansion that the company has delivered in recent years. Management called out their main priority as “building a new global tech platform” to manage their main business units in DoorDash, Deliveroo, and Wolt. My opinion is that this investment makes sense and may set the company up for rapid margin expansion potential beyond 2026 as it benefits from company synergies. Management reiterated their vision to “turn every physical retailer into an omni-channel player.” As a food delivery consumer, I have seen the competitive battle between DASH and Uber Eats (UBER). The fight for the delivery of retail items is looking like a battle for market share dominance, similar to what we previously saw in ridesharing between UBER and Lyft (LYFT).

Is DASH Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At first glance, DASH might not look buyable at all, with the stock trading at just around 32x forward earnings.

There are two key points here. The first is that the company is projected to sustain double-digit top-line growth over the coming years. I see continued upside in food delivery, but more importantly, consistent secular upside as the company reaches out to everything other than restaurants.

The second point is the business model quality. Those who have never used the product might view this to be the kind of luxury that goes away in a recession. As a loyal user of DoorDash, I am strongly of the opposite opinion. DASH offers a clear value proposition to all parties as it boosts business for restaurants, increases convenience for customers, and creates jobs for the “dashers.” Food delivery (and delivery of everything) is a business that arguably should have always existed, but it just hadn’t existed in earnest until this decade due to there not being the technology to match orders at scale. The key question is whether customers might prove promiscuous with their business, perhaps switching between DASH and UBER or other competitors based on pricing. I am a customer of both DASH and UBER, and I have the opinion that the fact that UBER has its mobility product in addition to delivery has not created sufficient reason to ditch my DASH services. This is an anecdotal datapoint, but DASH excels in terms of customer service, whereas it is very difficult (and frustrating) to even begin to troubleshoot any issues on UBER. I note that this sentiment appears to be shared by others as well.

Based on consensus estimates for around 16% to 18% forward growth (I note that 2026 consensus estimates are likely impacted by the acquisition of Deliveroo), the stock trades at around a 1.5x to 2.0x price to earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’). That PEG ratio declines if one believes that DASH can realize operating leverage over the long term, which I do. It is difficult to make a call for multiple expansion from here (that would need the stock to trade under a 1.2x PEG ratio), but I can see the stock delivering outsized returns from primarily the ongoing annual growth and also the GAAP earnings yield.

DASH Stock Risks

DASH is not cheap here, even after the recent underperformance. I currently view the business model as being of high quality, but it bears reminding that these “gig” companies had previously been widely seen as poster children of the 2021 bubble in tech stocks. It is possible that DASH sees its multiple compress, perhaps if it enters an aggressive pricing war with UBER or others. It is possible that Amazon (AMZN) eventually enters the competitive environment in a meaningful way. It is possible that local governments enact laws forcing the company to increase its driver compensation or operational cost structure.

DASH Stock Conclusion

Many pandemic winners saw their growth rates disappear following the pandemic. DASH bucked that trend and has continued to post strong double-digit growth even many years after. The company is now profitable on a GAAP basis, and I expect more margin expansion over the long term. I expect the company to see success in creating a new delivery market beyond restaurants. DASH has many of the traits that I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

data.title: Cracks Are Developing In The 'Everything' Bubble
data.description:
Blowout third quarter results from AI juggernaut NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) after the bell on Wednesday have halted the deterioration in the overall market here in November. At least temporarily. Combined with window dressing, the traditional 'Santa Claus Rally' and other seasonal factors, this might be enough to see equities at least trade flat or even end 2025 on a high note.

However, it does little to alleviate my concerns about the valuation levels of the equity market viewed from a historical lens (also known as key metrics investors that don't buy 'it is different this time' mantra use to evaluate the market). There are multiple bubbles within the market that have started to show some notable cracks recently. And equities were showing clear signs of faltering prior to NVIDIA's blowout Q3 numbers.

The AI Bubble

I covered this development again recently, so I won't rehash most of the points made in that article. However, there are two themes that do need to be revisited. The first is that big tech companies are increasingly moving to aggressive accounting measures such as depreciating GPUs on a five-year useful life schedule instead of three years.

This maneuver has the benefit of pumping up earnings and margins. However, if it is not accurate, it overstates earnings and understates long term capex and replacement costs. So, what is the true lifespan of a GPU working 24/7 in a massive data center? Two Years? Three Years? Does Moore's Law no longer apply? I have no idea what the right answer is to this question, other than it is highly unlikely the lifespan of a Blackwell GPU will end up being half a decade.

The other issue I think is understated is this. The massive amounts of money the tech giants are allocating to the AI buildout can no longer be funded from free cash flow and cash on the balance sheet. More companies like Oracle (ORCL) and Meta Platforms (META) have raised tens of billions of dollars via debt issuance. Special Purpose Vehicles for this purpose are flowering.

This is understandable. The projected capital expenditure budget for Amazon (AMZN) in FY2026 is roughly three times that of FY2023. The online retail and cloud services giant just raised $15 billion in its first bond deal since 2022. All this means much less cash available for stock buybacks as well as increasing interest costs. In early 2023, Meta Platforms announced a new $40 billion stock buyback authorization. Now it is issuing debt to finance its AI infrastructure plans.

How much will this impact earnings growth from Magnificent Seven in the coming quarters and years? What will the impact on overall profit growth of the market be, given that there is little earnings growth from the S&P 493?

The Cryptocurrency Bubble

One bubble that has started to pop over the past six to eight weeks is cryptocurrency. This reversal has not gotten that much attention in the financial press. Bitcoin has lost more than 25% of its value since it briefly went above $125,000 level on October 6th. Ethereum has seen a similar drop over the last three months. Less traded cryptocurrencies have seen steeper declines.

The Sovereign Debt Bubble

The federal debt just crossed the $38 trillion threshold. The U.S. debt to GDP ratio hovers around 120%. The need to refinance this gargantuan debt load is one reason that the average mortgage rate (tied primarily to the long treasury bond) is basically where it was in the summer of 2024, before the Federal Reserve started to cut rates by a total of 125 bps to date.

As bad as this debt bubble is in the United States, it is worse in other developed economies. Notably, Japan whose has the second oldest population in the world. The Land of the Rising Sun has a debt to GDP ratio more than twice that of the United States.

This is becoming more problematic as the yields on Japanese bonds have soared. The yield on the 30-year Japanese bonds stands around 3.4%, while the yield on 20-year debt is at 2.85%. These are the highest levels since June of 1999. This is a situation prudent investors should be paying close attention to, given its potential to disrupt the global debt markets.

The Housing Bubble

I returned to the moribund housing sector in this recent article. The most speculative part of the residential real estate market historically is condominiums. Weakness in this part of real estate tends to eventually bleed into the single-family sector. And as can be seen below, the bubble is in the process of popping in a significant way in the condominium market, especially in regions that experienced huge price appreciation following the COVID pandemic.

The CRE Bubble

We end with the bubble that is cracking in commercial real estate, or the CRE market. Office property values have already imploded in most major cities like Los Angeles, Chicago, and Houston from pre-pandemic levels. The explosion of the virtual workforce since COVID and rising costs for items like maintenance, insurance, utilities, and taxes with much higher vacancy rates have been a toxic combination for office values.

The delinquency rate for Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities, or CMBS, has roughly sextupled from their recent lows in 2022 against office properties. This was the year the Federal Reserve embarked on its most aggressive monetary policy since the days of Paul Volcker. CMBS delinquency rates are approaching 12%, higher than their peaks during the Great Financial Crisis. There is approximately $1.3 trillion of CRE debt outstanding against office properties.

The situation around multi-family properties is also deteriorating rapidly in a sector that has roughly $2.3 trillion of CRE debt outstanding. CMBS Delinquency rates have doubled over the prior 13 months to nearly seven percent. Multi-family properties are facing some of the same cost headwinds as owners of office properties in the form of rising insurance, tax, and maintenance expenses. All this while, rent growth is starting to flatline across the nation and is starting to fall in many regions of the country.

Obviously, given this outlook and wariness about the myriad bubbles that are apparent across the markets, my portfolio remains conservatively positioned. Perhaps surprisingly, 75% of my portfolio is within covered call positions around the few stocks I am finding in the market with reasonable valuations. This is higher than I would like given my market outlook. However, one of many things I have learned over four decades of investing is market bubbles can last much longer than they should. Alan Greenspan famously called equities 'irrational exuberant' in late 1996, more than three years before that massive bubble popped.

1% of my portfolio is in Armageddon trades that will deliver massive upside if the markets fall 20% to 30% over the next 12-15 months. The rest is in short-term treasuries and cash awaiting lower entry points to present themselves in the market.

data.title: Hyperscalers Are Starting To Question Massive AI Spending
data.description:
At any given time, there are literally hundreds of factors that drive the stock market. But there are always a few that are currently more important. Right now, the stock market is being heavily driven by three things: Tariffs, interest rates, and AI.

On Sept. 16, I wrote an article in Seeking Alpha titled How To Play The Coming AI Capex Bust. In it, I argued that while AI is here to stay and will continue to grow rapidly, the massive capex spending growth for data centers and GPUs will likely slow considerably by 2027.

Since then, numerous articles in Seeking Alpha and other publishers have echoed my thoughts. There have also been numerous AI industry CEOs who have echoed my thoughts to varying degrees. What's most concerning is that many of those CEOs are the same ones spending the most AI capex money.

Reasons for an AI Capex Bust

Before I get into more detail on CEO comments, let’s quickly revisit my reasons for calling current AI capex growth unsustainable.

  1. Future AI will need less power, as indicated by DeepSeek and soon Spiking Neural Networks.
  2. Power constraints will delay many data center projects for years.
  3. Efficiency gains will reduce the need to invest in infrastructure.
  4. New technologies, such as quantum computing, will take some business from data centers and lower costs.
  5. No proof of an acceptable ROI yet; returns for most are quite small so far.
  6. CFOs and investors will wait only so long for worthwhile payoffs.
  7. NIMBY pushback against data centers is picking up.
  8. New technology often takes longer than you think to pay off, as proven by the Internet in 2000 and autonomous driving (a form of AI) over the past 10 years.
  9. Numerous unsolved challenges in harnessing the power of the data centers to actually solve problems.
  10. Extreme AI-related stock valuations.
  11. Possible pushback from governments over workers losing jobs.

What The Spenders Are Saying

The majority of AI capex spending has been for data centers, and most of that has been by just four companies known as hyperscalers: Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META), and Microsoft (MSFT). OpenAI (OPENAI) is trying to join them, mentioning plans to invest a whopping $1 trillion in AI. But they do not have the balance sheet or cash flow to fund that at this time.

1. Alphabet

On Nov. 18, Alphabet Chief Executive Sundar Pichai, in an interview with the BBC, said no company would be unscathed if the AI boom collapses as ​soaring valuations and heavy investment in the sector fuel concerns of ‌a bubble.

He further said that the current wave ‌of AI investment was an "extraordinary moment" but acknowledged "elements of irrationality" in the market.

2. OpenAI

Sam Altman, in August, in an interview with The Verge, stated:

“The Internet was a really big deal. People got overexcited. Are we in a phase where investors as a whole are overexcited about AI? My opinion is yes.”

3. Meta

Meta has been one of the most aggressive spenders. In September, Mark Zuckerberg, in an interview on Access Podcast, said, “I do think that there’s definitely a possibility, at least empirically, based on past large infrastructure buildouts and how they led to bubbles, that something like that would happen here.”

On Oct. 21, 2025, Meta announced 600 AI-related job cuts. They noted that most of the staff affected will be shifted to other positions within Meta. This appears to be in relation to Meta’s launch of its Llama 4 AI language model in April, which has been unsuccessful to date. Reallocating AI jobs is a sign that ROI is becoming more important. This is critical because industry-wide spending to date has not taken ROI into account. Once that starts, returns need to be evident to continue spending growth.

A bigger deal may be an announcement by Meta the day before. Meta is building a massive new data center to be 80% owned by Blue Owl Capital. In the past, hyperscalers like Meta financed most of their data centers internally. By doing this transaction, Meta is removing some risk from its balance sheet. This is a sign of hedging their bets.

4. Microsoft

Microsoft, along with Palantir (PLTR), has arguably gotten the best returns on investment in AI so far, as witnessed by a recent acceleration in its cloud business. On a B2G podcast two weeks ago, CEO Satya Nadella took a different angle, saying:

“The biggest issue we are now having is not a compute glut, but it's power - it's sort of the ability to get the builds done fast enough close to power. So, if you can't do that, you may actually have a bunch of chips sitting in inventory that I can't plug in.”

5. Amazon

CEO Andy Jassy, in April 2025, on CNBC said the following:

“The cost of inference has come down several times, but it’s still more expensive than it should be, and it will be.”

“The lower we can make the cost of AI, the more customers will use it.”

For Amazon, like the others above, data centers are an expense, not a revenue source. They want to spend less on data centers.

6. Palantir

While not a hyperscaler, Palantir has been one of the most successful in using AI to increase revenues and earnings.

Palantir CEO Alex Karp, on Nov. 15, warned that in large swaths of the AI market, the cost to build the technology may not be worth it. He specifically said:

"One could argue as an informed citizen that the market is very large but may not create enough value to justify the actual cost of large language models or their implementation."

That comment appears to be a swipe at OpenAI.

Other very knowledgeable AI insiders have said similar things recently. For example, Peter Thiel's hedge fund sold all of its Nvidia (NVDA) stake last quarter.

Many others have raised the alarm since my article, but the hyperscalers are those who matter most. That’s because they are the primary spenders right now.

Other issues popping up are things like circular financing. A major example of that is Nvidia investing in its customers’ stock and projects. Another issue is that capex spending for the hyperscalers is reaching a point where it's using up most of their cash flow. There are increasing power constraints. It takes a long time to get approvals and then build a new power plant. Adding electricity demand to the current grid could lead to more power outages and more costs to consumers.

Why It Matters

I said at the beginning of this article that AI was one of three factors moving the stock market right now. Of those, AI, in my opinion, is the biggest tailwind holding up the economy. In my September article, I provided the math behind my estimate that AI capex growth represents one-third to one-half of U.S. GDP growth this year. I may be underestimating it, as that is just from direct revenues and excludes indirect.

I believe worries of an AI slowdown impacted the stock market over the past five days, specifically from the recent comments by Karp and Pichai.

Takeaway

Note that this article is about AI capex spending on things like data centers and GPUs, which has reached more than $300 billion this year. It's not a comment on AI itself, which should continue to grow rapidly.

Nvidia just reported 3Q earnings, and its growth remains strong with no sign of stopping, at least this quarter. But when the most aggressive AI spenders are warning about an AI shakeout, investors need to listen. I maintain my prediction of a significant AI capex growth slowdown by 2027. But we will likely see signs of that beforehand.

Keep watching the hyperscalers. They're starting to talk caution but haven't acted on it yet. Once they do, avoid those companies that derive significant revenue from data center construction.

data.title: Rivian: Path To Profitability
data.description:
Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN) reported better-than-expected earnings for its September quarter at the beginning of the month, due to a boost in revenue in both key segments. The EV company trimmed its full-year delivery outlook in October, however, and now expects to deliver only between 41,500 and 43,500 electric vehicles compared to a previous outlook of up to 46,000 EVs. Rivian saw a sequential improvement in its gross margin profile, and the company should be able to boost its deliveries next year as the R2 EV becomes available.

While sales may be weaker in Q4 due to expiring EV credits in the September quarter, I see a path for higher deliveries in FY 2026 as well as to profitability for Rivian long term. I previously rated shares of Rivian a hold, in large part because the EV maker benefited from improving unit economics in its core manufacturing business. I maintain my hold rating following the Q3 earnings report.

Q3 Earnings Review

Rivian reported better-than-expected results on both the top and bottom lines for the September quarter on November 4, 2025: in Q3, the EV enterprise had normalized earnings of $(0.65) per share, which exceeded the Wall Street estimate by $0.07 per share. Rivian’s revenue figure was reported at $1.56B, outperforming the consensus estimate by $47.6M.

Strong Production Rebound Driven By Expiring Tax Credits

Rivian delivered 13,201 electric vehicles during the July-September period, which highlighted a growth rate of 32% year-over-year. Compared to the second-quarter, deliveries increased 24% Q/Q in part because consumers pulled electric vehicle purchases forward in order to be eligible for EV tax credits that expired at the end of September.

The absence of EV subsidies is highly likely to lead to a dip in revenue growth for Rivian in the fourth-quarter, as well as for other EV companies, and it is one reason why the company trimmed its guidance for FY 2025.

The EV maker saw a significant rebound in its production volume to 10,720 units as well in Q3 as well (+79% Q/Q) as previous supplier snags were resolved. The trend in deliveries is generally a positive one for Rivian, with delivery volumes rising consistently throughout the year. About ~76% of the company's full-year delivery target, at its mid-point (41,500-43,500), has been fulfilled as of the end of the September quarter, and I expect the company to reach its guidance for FY 2025.

The EV company achieved $1.56B in total revenue in the third-quarter, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 78%. While the automotive segment did well with 47.2% growth in Q3'25, it was Rivian's Software & Services segment that was mainly responsible for this sharp up-swing in revenue.

In the third-quarter, this segment, which consolidates results from Rivian's partnership with Volkswagen, generated 325% growth compared to last year and total revenue of $416M. This segment has become significantly more important for Rivian in the last year, with the percentage revenue share rising from 11% in Q3'24 to 27% in Q3'25.

Volkswagen and Rivian partnered last year -- Volkswagen Deal Is A Game Changer -- in order to jointly develop electrical architecture for electric vehicles. As part of this development partnership, Rivian contributes vehicle electrical architecture and software development services to the joint venture with Volkswagen, payments totaling $214M (~51% of total revenue). The total value of these payments is going to be $5.8B (by FY 2027), according to the JV agreement, which could drastically help Rivian accelerate its path to profitability within the next 2–3 years.

$millions Q3'24 Q4'24 Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Y/Y Growth
Revenue $874 $1,734 $1,240 $1,303 $1,558 78.3%
Automotive $776 $1,520 $922 $927 $1,142 47.2%
% Of Total 89% 88% 74% 71% 73% -17.4%
Software & Services $98 $214 $318 $376 $416 324.5%
% Of Total 11% 12% 26% 29% 27% 138.1%

Source: Author

Path To Profitability For Rivian, Decreasing Cash Burn

Rivian trimmed its FY 2025 delivery outlook amid slowing demand for its R1 product line-up. Currently, the EV maker expects 41,500-43,500 electric vehicles to be delivered this year vs. a previous outlook of 40,000-46,000 units. I expect a significant increase in production and delivery volume for Rivian in 2026 as the company is slated to bring to market the more affordable R2 -- a mid-size, five-seat electric SUV expected to retail for ~$45,000 -- in the first half of FY 2026.

In the third-quarter, Rivian generated a gross profit of $24M, showing a $416M year-over-year swing. In Q3'25, Rivian's gross profit per vehicle sold was $2,239 compared to a gross loss of $34,450 per unit in the previous quarter. The improving gross profit profile is mainly due to the company rolling off more vehicles from its factory belts in the third-quarter.

Rivian posted its third quarter within the last trailing-twelve months period in which the EV maker reported positive gross margins... so the business seems generally on the right track. The gross profit (margin) trajectory also looks much better for Rivian than it does for Lucid (LCID), a key rival in the (premium) EV segment in the U.S., mainly because Rivian has a larger production base. Lucid's gross losses are still skyhigh, and the company losses about as much money on a gross profit basis as it brings in revenue.

Rivian, $M Q3'24 Q4'24 Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Y/Y Growth
Revenue $874 $1,734 $1,240 $1,303 $1,558 78.3%
Gross Profit -$392 $170 $206 -$206 $24 -106.1%
Gross Profit Margin -45% 10% 17% -16% 2% -104.4%
Lucid, $M Q3'24 Q4'24 Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Y/Y Growth
Revenue $200 $234 $235 $259 $337 68.5%
Gross Profit -$213 -$209 -$229 -$273 -$334 57.0%
Gross Profit Margin -106% -89% -97% -105% -99% -6.8%

Source: Author

Besides a better gross margin profile, Rivian has a stronger balance sheet as well... although both EV companies have considerable access to liquidity. Rivian has about 3X more cash and investments on its balance sheet than Lucid, with total cash and short-term investments amounting to $7.1B as of the end of the September quarter.

In terms of cash burn, Rivian is still losing money and will likely continue to do so for at least two more years: it had about $421M in negative free cash flow in the September quarter (+6% Q/Q). Given the firm's massive cash balance, however, I currently calculate that Rivian has a liquidity run-way of 12–14 quarters. While the R2 ramp in H1'26 is likely to lead to higher operating expenses in the short-term, Rivian has significantly reduced its cash burn over time: in the year-earlier period, Rivian burned through $1.15B in cash, showing a year-over-year decline rate of 63%.

Rivian has a path to profitability if it can grow its deliveries on the back of new product releases, especially if the company's R2 SUV is met with success in the marketplace next year. I believe the EV enterprise could sell 20-30k R2 units in 2026, which could yield ~70,000 total EV sales next year. This implies 65% growth potential from the mid-point guidance of 42,500 EV units this year. The company has guided for 68k reservations immediately after reservations opened last year, and since Rivian has already established mass-production capacity for its SUVs, the company should be able to rapidly ramp of production and deliveries. According to Rivian's VP of Manufacturing, the R2 had more than 100,000 reservations in mid-2024, and numbers have likely risen way beyond this since. My estimate of 20-30k R2 unit deliveries is, therefore, in my opinion, a very conservative estimate.

Rivian's Illinois manufacturing facility has a 150k unit annual production capacity, but the EV company is expanding its footprint by adding 1.1 million-square-foot to its plant, which will lift the total output capacity to 215k units.

While Rivian is losing more money than Lucid right now (on a net income basis), the company's gross margin trend, ramp in Software & Services revenue and lower cash burn are reasons why I see an improving profitability profile for Rivian, especially in FY 2026 which is when the firm will start to generate a new revenue and income stream from the R2.

Rivian: Potentially Best Deal In The EV Industry Group

The EV company is currently valued at a forward price-to-revenue ratio of 2.7X. Lucid is currently valued at a 1.9X forward revenue multiplier, but I feel the EV company is by far the riskiest investment of the two, given its high gross losses and lack of significant margin improvement. On an enterprise-value-to-revenue basis, Rivian is also much cheaper than Lucid: 3.0X vs. 4.7X.

Given that Rivian is on the brink of broadening its product portfolio, the company has also much lower execution risks and should be able to go full-steam into the R2 ramp in the first half of FY 2026. While Lucid also has a strong balance sheet, the company has unsustainable gross margins... which makes the EV company especially risky.

In the last three years, Rivian had an average P/S ratio of 2.9X, which is 7% higher than the firm's current valuation. If Rivian's R2 becomes a success and the company can escalate its delivery growth in FY 2026, while growing its gross margins on higher volume, I see a path for a revaluation to a ~2.9X P/S ratio. This re-valuation, however, is contingent on Rivian successively escalating its delivery pace and lowering its operating losses. Therefore, I see a fair value of $16 for Rivian's shares, which translates to a hold rating.

Market Cap Next Year's Est. Revenues P/S Ratio 2026 EPS (est.) EV/S
Rivian $18.5B $6.91B 2.7 X -$0.03 3.0 X
Lucid $4.6B $2.43B 1.9 X -$6.59 4.7X

Source: Author

Risks With Rivian

One risk for the EV company is a potential slowdown in electric vehicle deliveries in Q4 due to an expected dip in sales due to the expiration of the EV tax credit. What would change my mind about Rivian is if the EV maker were to incur a higher cash burn that shortened the firm's liquidity run-way below 12–14 months (as I estimated).

The general trend in gross margins is positive for Rivian and should continue to benefit the EV maker as it ramps its production and delivery volume. However, disappointing delivery numbers in FY 2026 as well as a poor market reception for the R2 SUV (unlikely, in my opinion, given reservation stats) could potentially hurt the company's ability to scale... which would be a negative development for my bullish-leaning thesis.

Closing Thoughts

Rivian exceeded expectations for the third-quarter on both the top and bottom lines, which caused shares to soar as high as $18. The surge in revenue was due to two main factors: 1) Consumers have pulled their EV purchases forward in order to qualify for the expiring $7,500 EV tax credit, and 2) The Software & Services segment, which benefited from JV-related payments.

The gross profit trend for Rivian is looking very good, and the company did not lose any money per-vehicle sold in Q3'25, on a gross profit basis. Plus, Rivian was profitable in terms of gross profits in 3 out of the last 4 quarters. The gross profit trend for Rivian also looks much better than for Lucid, which struggles with higher operating costs and less scale in its manufacturing footprint... which makes Rivian comparatively a more promising investment, in my opinion.

Rivian also has a cashed up balance sheet with $7.1B in immediate liquidity, which drastically limits investors’ risks and ensures that the company has sufficient cash to finance the ramp of new EV products.

data.title: Nayax: Q3 Reinforces The Bull Case
data.description:
Nayax Ltd. (NYAX) is down slightly since my previous two ratings, but I think that’s more of a visibility problem than a fundamental one. The company dominates in a non-exciting corner of the payments space. The Q3 results, though, reveal a company with very good underlying operations. The improving margin profile and predictable recurring revenue base lend more support to the higher end of the P/FCF multiple range I used in my previous article. I am therefore maintaining my Buy rating on Nayax.

Recurring Revenue is King

Nayax’s recurring revenue engine is built on Payment Processing and SaaS fees, and this part of the business has grown linearly since 2017. In Q3, recurring revenue reached $77.1 million, or 73.9% of total revenue. While that share is healthy, I would actually prefer to see it rise at a slower pace. The hardware business, which is non-recurring, expands the installed base that later feeds recurring revenue. If the mix skews too far toward recurring, say north of 90%, it becomes harder to sustain 20% plus growth rates because the hardware funnel narrows.

The evolving mix inside Payment Processing is also worth highlighting. Nayax is gaining traction in higher-ticket verticals like EV charging, amusement, and car washes. These categories carry much larger transaction sizes compared to classic vending. For example, an EV charge is $18 on DC and $3-$4 on AC, whereas the average ticket size on a vending machine is around $2.24. That gives the business a natural lift. Companies rarely affirm targets going 3 years in the future. But Nayax did confirm its target to grow revenue by 35% annually through 2028. I think this changing mix is likely feeding into these projections.

Improving Margin Profile

We are also seeing the effects of scale and operating leverage taking hold on Nayax’s margin profile. Gross margins expanded 360 basis points year-over-year to 49.3%, a major contributor to the 420-basis point increase in adjusted EBITDA margin (17.5%). Management attributed it to the development of a smart routing system that channels payments through the cheapest payment processors. The scale of processing over $1.7 billion quarterly also gives it the bargaining power to negotiate better terms with banks.

Nayax’s 2028 targets stipulate an adjusted EBITDA margin of 30%, with 50% of that converting to cash flows. A few months ago, one would have wondered if management was a tad too rosy with that projection. The margin improvement in the first two quarters wasn't very impressive. But the Q3 progression fit better with the linear step change observed since 2022. So if this is durable, it needs to hit a similar cadence of 400 basis point improvement annually going into 2028 to hit its targets.

Valuation and Risks

I consider both the factors discussed above as significant factor contributors for maintaining a high multiple. Here’s why:

A valuation multiple is a proxy for risk and future growth profile. In a transactional business, if you start at $0 every period, you must essentially “rewin” your revenue. Effectively to double your revenue, you must double your sales effort. On a recurring basis, new sales are added to an existing stack, which allows a company to increase revenue without a linear increase in sales and marketing costs. So, a recurring base does both—lower the risk and accelerate the future growth potential.

Margins, on the other hand, act as a litmus test for your moat. If you can charge a high price relative to costs, it implies customers love your product or there are few substitutes for what you offer. In my first rating on NYAX, I had concluded that the company does have a moat, and a low churn rate of 2.8% and dollar-based retention rate of 122% again support that view.

Based on the 2028 targets, the company expects to generate $150 million in cash flows, and I have been using a 12-20x multiple range for my price targets. A continuation of the trend in margin enhancements would push it closer to the higher end of the range. The company's diluted shares outstanding have increased slightly to 38.4 million, so the average 2028 FCF/share comes out to $3.91.

In terms of risks, it is important to note that Nayax expects 10% of its 35% CAGR through 2028 to come from inorganic growth. The payments industry is quite fragmented, and this strategy to accelerate market share brings integration risk. Fiserv (FISV) is a case study in this regard, and merging disparate technology stacks is especially challenging within the industry.

Additionally, the market is a bit frothy right now, and asset prices are elevated. There is a risk that Nayax feels pressured to hit its growth targets and ends up overpaying for an acquisition. It remains to be seen if it can see through capital allocation discipline, as growth at the wrong price destroys shareholder value. And if you combine it with technical debt from integration risks, you have a recipe for failure.

Conclusion

The Q3 results validate the bull case for Nayax Ltd. Recurring revenue is a dominant driver of growth, and operating leverage is materializing on the bottom line. While M&A integration risks in the future exist, the underlying fundamentals are too strong to ignore at this point in time. I am therefore maintaining my Buy rating on Nayax.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Nvidia Lands Counterpunch On AI Naysayers
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Wall Street analysts loved what they saw from Nvidia earnings. (0:15) Long-awaited September jobs report shows solid payroll gains. (1:10) Walmart benefits as consumers eye value. (2:21)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Nvidia (NVDA) is iis in the spotlight.

Wall Street is echoing with the praise of analysts, who say the GPU giant’s results and guidance indicate it is “firing on all cylinders” and is a counterpunch for nearly every bearish take on the AI spending boom.

Morgan Stanley analyst Joseph Moore gave CEO Jensen Huang for addressing the AI bubble concerns, saying his “explanations for wanting to enable the growth in the ecosystem, the useful lives of its products, and ultimately the value proposition for AI investments resonate.”

Jefferies analyst Blayne Curtis pointsout that Nvidia “answered the bell,” amid the recent crop up in concerns related to AI demand, financing, and GPU lifespan and deprecation.

Evercore ISI’s Mark Lipacis says simply NVDA is the “AI ecosystem of choice.”

And, unsurprisingly, Wedbush’s Dan Ives hyped up the hyperscaler hyperbole said Jensen’s earnings call was “heard round the world.”

But while Nvidia looks good on the fundamentals, it looks too soon to say the AI trade is back. The sell-the-rip crowd hit both the stock and the overall market all morning.

On the economic front, the long-awaited jobs number from September. After the government shutdown kept the data on the sidelines, the BLS reported a strong rise of 119,000 in nonfarm payrolls.

That was more than double expectations but tempered by downward revisions of 33,000 for July and August. And perhaps more importantly for the Fed, the unemployment rose to 4.4%, the highest level in four years.

Wells Fargo economists say as that today's report seems likely to be the last needle-mover for the FOMC ahead of its December meeting.

“Today’s report does not change our minds on what the FOMC should do. Another 25-bps rate cut seems warranted, they said, “amid a still sluggish pace of job growth, a nearly 4.5% unemployment rate and a still restrictive policy rate setting.”

“That said, what the Fed will do is a separate question and debate. Our sense is that today's reports will give the hawks enough ammunition to stick to their position of no hike in December.”

On that note, we’ll move bump up the Wall Street Research Corner a little early, because Societe Generale strategist and self-described Uber Bear Albert Edwards says ignore Nvidia's results and delayed U.S. economic data.

That’s because "the single most important development in markets at the moment is surging long #JGB yields ... The 10-year has broken out and jumped to 1.82% today."

Edwards asks: "Is this what breaks the equity bull market?"

He said back in May that spiking Japanese bond yields are the most significant risk to global markets since the 2008 crisis-era regime of bond-yield suppression began.

Japan has historically been an early warning point for major financial turns and Japanese institutions could pull capital home, reversing years of yen-funded carry trades and heavy foreign bond buying, including U.S. Treasuries.

Among active stocks, Walmart’s (WMT) is popping after Q3 results and guidance underscored its proficiency at maintaining a strong value proposition as consumers rein in spending.

Management reaffirmed its focus on delivering profit growth that outpaces sales and Evercore ISI says the chain is “better positioned than most retailers to navigate the choppy tariff and consumer backdrop”

Bath & Body Works (BBWI) is plunging after the retailer fell short of Q3 estimates and lowered its outlook for the remainder of the year.

CEO Daniel Heaf says a transformation plan will position the business for sustainable, long-term growth. But "it will take time for the impact to be reflected in our financial performance."

And Verizon (VZ) has begun a massive layoff process expected to impact more than 13,000 employees, including retail workers, customer service representatives, and some senior executives.

According to the report, CEO Dan Schulman said in a memo Verizon’s current cost structure “limits our ability to invest significantly in our customer value proposition.”

In other news of note, Meta (META) has started contacting teenage users in Australia, warning them to download their account data before the social media ban for those under 16 takes effect.

Australia will ban users under 16 from December 10. Platforms including Meta’s Facebook and Instagram and TikTok (TIKTOK) (BDNCE), X, YouTube (GOOG) (GOOGL), Snapchat (SNAP), Reddit (RDDT) and Kick.

Meta contacted thousands of young account holders through SMS and email saying that users under the age limit will start to be denied access from December 4, and they may “save their contacts and memories” ahead of the ban and update their contact information “so we can get in touch and help them regain access once they turn 16.”

The company estimates there are 350,000 Australians on Instagram and 150,000 on Facebook between 13 and 15.

data.title: Alger Mid Cap 40 ETF Q3 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Alger Mid Cap 40 ETF Q3 2025 Commentary.

Portfolio Update

The Alger Mid Cap 40 ETF outperformed the Russell Midcap Growth Index during the third quarter of 2025. AppLovin Corp. (APP), Talen Energy Corp (TLN), and Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD), were among the top contributors to performance.

  • AppLovin is an advertising technology company offering a digital platform that helps mobile app developers market, monetize, and analyze their apps. We believe the company is experiencing a positive lifecycle change, driven by its AI-powered software engine. While currently focused on mobile gaming, AppLovin is expanding into other market segments. Its Demand Side Platform (DSP) supports ad placements, user acquisition, inventory matching, and performance analytics. Further, we believe AI is central to AppLovin’s growth, driving a large majority of the company’s revenue through its recommendation and targeting engine. In our view, AppLovin gains a competitive advantage, delivering higher-value app installs by leveraging data from its game portfolio and developer partners. We believe this scale and data advantage enhances its network effect, improving its technology and boosting market share in mobile gaming. As developers use the platform, the company collects data that continuously refines its algorithms. Shares contributed positively to performance during the period due to strong operating results, which highlighted continued growth in its advertising platform and software business. Momentum further accelerated in September when AppLovin was added to the S&P 500 Index, a milestone that enhanced visibility and underscored the company’s growing market presence.
  • Talen Energy is an independent power producer operating in the PJM market across the Mid-Atlantic and Midwest, where constrained supply and rising demand from data centers running AI workloads—particularly in northern Virginia—are supporting higher power prices and capacity. Talen also leverages its Cumulus digital infrastructure platform, which includes a hyperscale data center campus adjacent to its Susquehanna nuclear facility, providing carbon-free, low-cost energy directly from the plant and creating incremental revenue opportunities. While fiscal second-quarter operating results were modestly below estimates due to extended outages at Susquehanna, management reaffirmed full-year guidance, reinforcing visibility around future demand and grid revenues. The company also hosted an investor day in September, where management introduced its 2027 and 2028 outlook, which served as a positive catalyst for the stock.
  • Robinhood is a consumer-focused financial platform offering commission-free trading in stocks, options, futures and crypto, alongside retirement investing and subscription benefits via Robinhood Gold. In 2025 the company also broadened into wealth management with Robinhood Strategies (expert-managed portfolios), and announced Robinhood Banking and Robinhood Cortex as part of a wider push to deliver advisory, banking and AI-driven tools within the app. With over 25 million accounts, Robinhood holds roughly 15–20% market share among brokerage accounts, but only about 0.3% of the $70 trillion in U.S. household investable assets, according to management. Shares contributed positively to relative performance during the period after the company reported strong operating results, with revenues and earnings beating analyst estimates.

Wingstop, Inc. (WING), Axon Enterprise Inc (AXON), and Twilio, Inc. (TWLO), were among the top detractors from performance.

  • Wingstop is a global restaurant brand best known for its cooked- to-order, hand-sauced chicken wings. The company operates just over 2,000 locations worldwide, with most in the United States. Wingstop delivered strong fiscal second-quarter results, exceeding expectations despite facing tough comparisons from prior years. Sales momentum was supported by several factors, including new menu offerings, increased marketing, and continued growth in digital ordering, all of which have boosted brand awareness and profitability. However, shares declined later in the quarter following reports of softer sales trends, as the restaurant industry has experienced a growth slowdown due to broad based consumer price aversion and a rotation towards food-at-home. Despite the near-term industry slowdown, we continue to view Wingstop favorably for its long-term growth potential and near-term catalysts, including the rollout of Smart Kitchen initiatives and an enhanced loyalty program.
  • Axon Enterprise is a leading provider of public safety technology spanning TASER conducted-energy devices, body-worn and in-car cameras, and cloud software that manages digital evidence and incident workflows for law-enforcement agencies. The company serves the vast majority of U.S. departments (≈18,000 agencies) and is broadening adoption globally as customers standardize on its integrated hardware-plus-software platform (e.g., TASER 10, Body 4, and Axon Cloud/Evidence.com). We believe Axon’s installed base, recurring software mix, and rapid product cadence create a durable, compounding model with multiple avenues for growth: (1) continued refresh cycles in TASER and sensors; (2) expanding cloud subscriptions embedded in agency workflows; and (3) increasing contribution from AI-enabled features that improve report accuracy, reduce time spent on paperwork, and elevate operational efficiency. Despite delivering better-than-expected fiscal second-quarter earnings, shares detracted from performance as strong year-to-date gains and a premium valuation left the stock vulnerable to profit-taking. The pressure intensified in late September when Axon announced plans to acquire an AI-powered 911 communications platform, prompting concerns about expense intensity and near-term spending. While the stock experienced a short-term pullback, we continue to view Axon favorably, showcasing enterprise AI in action with publicly disclosed time-savings data. Management commentary and robust bookings further reinforce our conviction in the company’s AI-driven cloud momentum and long-term growth outlook.
  • Twilio is a leading provider of Communications Platform as a Service (CPaaS), enabling enterprises to embed messaging, voice, and other real-time communication capabilities into their applications without building complex infrastructure. For example, text alerts from a bank or automated appointment reminders from a doctor’s office are often powered by Twilio. Its platform combines conversational AI, real-time analytics, and compliance features. Strategic partnerships and continued innovation reinforce Twilio’s role in this evolving ecosystem. During the quarter, shares declined despite solid second-quarter results, which included double-digit revenue growth and record free cash flow. The pullback reflected management’s outlook for slower third-quarter profit growth and gross margin pressure from a heavier mix of messaging traffic and rising carrier fees, as well as tougher year-over-year comparisons.

Risk Disclosures: Investing in the stock market involves risks, including the potential loss of principal. Growth stocks may be more volatile than other stocks as their prices tend to be higher in relation to their companies’ earnings and may be more sensitive to market, political, and economic developments. Investing in companies of medium capitalizations involves the risk that such issuers may have limited product lines or financial resources, lack management depth, or have limited liquidity. A significant portion of assets may be invested in securities of companies in related sectors, and may be similarly affected by economic, political, or market events and conditions and may be more vulnerable to unfavorable sector developments. Assets may be focused in a small number of holdings, making them susceptible to risks associated with a single economic, political or regulatory event than a more diversified portfolio. Active trading may increase transaction costs, brokerage commissions, and taxes, which can lower the return on investment. At times, cash may be a larger position in the portfolio and may underperform relative to equity securities. ETF shares are based on market price rather than net asset value (“NAV”), as a result, shares may trade at a price greater than NAV (a premium) or less than NAV (a discount). The Fund may also incur brokerage commissions, as well as the cost of the bid/ask spread, when purchasing or selling ETF shares. The Fund faces numerous market trading risks, including the potential lack of an active market for Fund shares, losses from trading in secondary markets, periods of high volatility and disruption in the creation and/or redemption process of the Fund. Any of these factors, among others, may lead to the Fund’s shares trading at a premium or discount to NAV. Thus, you may pay more (or less) than NAV when you buy shares of the Fund in the secondary market, and you may receive less (or more) than NAV when you sell those shares in the secondary market. The Manager cannot predict whether shares will trade above (premium), below (discount) or at NAV. Certain shareholders, including other funds advised by the Manager or an affiliate of the Manager, may from time to time own a substantial amount of the shares of the Fund. Redemptions by large shareholders could have a significant negative impact on the Fund. Companies involved in, or exposed to, AI-related businesses may have limited product lines, markets, financial resources or personnel as they face intense competition and potentially rapid product obsolescence, and many depend significantly on retaining and growing their consumer base. These companies may be substantially exposed to the market and business risks of other industries or sectors, and may be adversely affected by negative developments impacting those companies, industries or sectors, as well as by loss or impairment of intellectual property rights or misappropriation of their technology. Companies that utilize AI could face reputational harm, competitive harm, and legal liability, and/or an adverse effect on business operations as content, analyses, or recommendations that AI applications produce may be deficient, inaccurate, biased, misleading or incomplete, may lead to errors, and may be used in negligent or criminal ways. AI companies, especially smaller companies, tend to be more volatile than companies that do not rely heavily on technology. Investing in innovation is not without risk and there is no guarantee that investments in research and development will result in a company gaining market share or achieving enhanced revenue. Companies exploring new technologies may face regulatory, political or legal challenges that may adversely impact their competitive positioning and financial prospects. Developing technologies to displace older technologies or create new markets may not in fact do so, and there may be sector-specific risks. There will be winners and losers Fred Alger Management, LLC uses the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS®) for categorizing companies into sectors and industries.

Fred Alger Management, LLC uses the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS®) for categorizing companies into sectors and industries. GICS® is used for all portfolio characteristics involving sector and industry data such as benchmark, active and relative weights and attribution. Sector and industry classifications are sourced from GICS. Historical classifications use GICS categories available as of the date of this presentation.

The indices presented are provided for illustrative purposes, reflect the reinvestment of dividends and do not assess fees and expenses that would have the effect of reducing returns. Investors cannot invest directly in any index. The index performance does not represent the returns of any portfolio advised by Fred Alger Management, LLC and actual client results might differ materially than the indices shown. The performance data quoted represents past performance, which is not an indication or a guarantee of future results.

S&P 500® Index: An index of large company stocks considered to be representative of the U.S. stock market. Index performance does not reflect deductions for fees, expenses, or taxes. Russell Midcap® Growth Index: Measures the performance of the mid-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. Index performance does not reflect deductions for fees, expenses, or taxes. After March 24, 2025, FTSE Russell implemented a new methodology capping individual companies at no more than 22.5% of the index and capping companies that have a weight greater than 4.5% in aggregate at no more than 45% of the index. The following positions represented the noted percentages of portfolio assets as of September 30, 2025: AppLovin Corp. Class A, 8.58%; Talen Energy Corp, 4.72%; Robinhood Markets, Inc. Class A, 5.08%; Wingstop, Inc., 1.98%; Axon Enterprise Inc, 2.96%; and Twilio, Inc. Class A, 0%.
